feedback.text: The digital fairness act should maybe look into extending protections to video game consumers as well, as they are becoming a more digital service, rather than having physical items. Recently there has been "online drama" over game developers or publishers removing games that are designed to earn money over its lifetime when the income is reduced to below what is profitable. While I don't think they should be forced to keep online services available at a loss, I do feel that them giving tools for fans of games being removed to be able to continue to play the game that they have actually paid for. Examples giving out tools for fans to set up their own servers, or for games that require an online connection, but are in essence made for a single-player experience, to still be playable offline regardless of the developer or publisher's wish to keep stats about their game, or their users. There is already a European Citizens initiative with over one million signatures, which I am not able to partake in as a non-EU citizen, but my country (Norway) adopts EU regulation into its own laws.
